<br />
<br />
<br />
<br />
<div class="container-fluid main-container">




<div id="header">



<h1 class="title toc-ignore">Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python
and SQL</h1>
<h4 class="author">Darrell Wolfe</h4>

</div>


<div id="section" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
<div id="disclaimers-first" class="section level1">
<h1>Disclaimers First</h1>
<p>It is not my intention to steal anyone’s thunder or copyrighted
material. I do not believe these seven fundamentals are specific to Dr
Hill (below), who was the initial inspiration to start this note file.
That beings said, she has a particularly unique method of teaching, and
I strongly recommend that if you are someone who needs a good teacher,
she’s the one!</p>
<p>This is my own process of learning. I take information from as many
sources and teachers as possible, synthesis that material, and then
practice it until I get good at it. Further, I like to take detailed
notes so I can refer back to them when a particular tool starts getting
rusty or dusty in my brain after disuse for a time.</p>
<p>When I learned .rmd through my Google Data Analytics Certification, I
started incorporating that into my note taking (previously in OneNote or
Notion).</p>
<p>Therefore it is unlikely you will hear anything from today that is
unique.</p>
<p>Further, this is not intended to be a for-profit article (but if I
can eventually add affiliate links I will, because who doesn’t want to
get paid for writing?). If I do add any links, affiliate or not, they
are only those I found most helpful as I was learning and putting this
together.</p>
<p>Also, this is my own personal notes that I’m sharing, so it may
change over time as I learn more.</p>
<div id="section-1" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="prologue" class="section level1">
<h1>Prologue</h1>
<p>This project was inspired by the The Six Figure Software Developer
Masterclass webinar by Dr Emily Hill, which I took (today as I write
this intro) on March 30, 2024. If you get a chance to attend her
webinars I highly recommend it. Hers was the only webinar I’ve ever
found helpful and worth attending.</p>
<p>Details: <a href="https://joyofcoding.academy/" class="uri">https://joyofcoding.academy/</a></p>
<p>When I can gather the funds to attend her full bootcamp program, I
will probably do so. However, my funds are too tight for the expense at
this time.</p>
<p>She mentioned that if one understands the seven basics of coding, one
can build on those concepts inside any given programming language. The
idea being, that if you understand these concepts, the application of
the concepts will apply across languages, even if the syntax (exact
wording structure) changes from language to language.</p>
<p>I found this to be the case in my experience. I began in Excel in
2010, and over the years began mastering more and more complicated Excel
formulas. In 2022-2023, I attended a course taught by David Cornell
(below), where I learned Power Query, DAX modeling, and Power BI. In the
following year I used ChatGPT (as a tutor, not a code writer) and W3
Schools to learn SQL. I found that SQL had extensive crossover
conceptually with Excel formulas/DAX.</p>
<p>I started Python in Coursera and discovered that the concepts
crossover there too. I also had some (ancient) experience with HTML/CSS
and those concepts all crossover.</p>
<p>With the framework of thinking of coding as seven basic fundamentals
(which I heard today), I think I can build on this understanding for
myself. I asked ChatGPT-4 to write a course plan based on these 7
concepts, and then asked it to write the individual weeks.</p>
<p>Starting from that skelleton, I am building this document to which I
will add anything else I learn.</p>
<div id="section-2" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="resources" class="section level1">
<h1>Resources</h1>
<p>I want to list the tools, websites, and authors who help me as I
build this document (learning as I go), so these are links to the
important sites to bookmark for yourself. Incidentally, I am writing
this is RStudio, in an .rmd file type with r markdown. It then exports
to HTML which I will host on my site for final consumption and
sharing.</p>
<ul>
<li>RStudio - <a href="https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/" class="uri">https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/</a></li>
<li>Joy of Coding (Website) - <a href="https://joyofcoding.academy/" class="uri">https://joyofcoding.academy/</a></li>
<li>Joy of Coding (YouTube) - <a href="https://www.youtube.com/@joyofcoding4all" class="uri">https://www.youtube.com/@joyofcoding4all</a></li>
<li>ChatGPT-4 - <a href="https://chat.openai.com/" class="uri">https://chat.openai.com/</a></li>
<li>W3 Schools (Python) - <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/python/default.asp" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/python/default.asp</a></li>
<li>W3 Schools (SQL) - <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/sql/default.asp" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/sql/default.asp</a></li>
<li>W3 Schools (r) - <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/r/default.asp" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/r/default.asp</a></li>
<li>Alex the Analyst - <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rGx1QNdYzvs&amp;list=PLUaB-1hjhk8FE_XZ87vPPSfHqb6OcM0cF" class="uri">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rGx1QNdYzvs&amp;list=PLUaB-1hjhk8FE_XZ87vPPSfHqb6OcM0cF</a></li>
<li>R Prograing 101 - R programming in one hour - a crash course for
beginners - <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eR-XRSKsuR4" class="uri">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eR-XRSKsuR4</a></li>
<li>Cornell Consultants, DAVID CORNELL, MAI, CAE - <a href="https://www.cornellconsultants.com/about.html" class="uri">https://www.cornellconsultants.com/about.html</a></li>
</ul>
<div id="section-3" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="introduction" class="section level1">
<h1>Introduction</h1>
<p>Throughout my life, I’ve floated from one project to another,
acquiring skill and knowledge enough to feel a minimum mastery over a
given topic. I get to a certain point, lose interest, and move on to the
next topic. I changed my major at least five times during my six years
of junior college (1998-2004). I was going to be a Theater major, but I
never got around to taking a theater course after highschool. I
transitioned to Music, English, History, and then IT.</p>
<p>I dropped out to have kids, went back to school as an adult
(2019-2022) and majored in Biblical Studies, while also training to
become a Business Intelligence Analyst (BIA). How’s that for a
convoluted career path?</p>
<p>I always called myself a “Jack of all trades”, as a self effacing put
down because I could never stick to one thing long enough to grow in
it.</p>
<p>Then I learned I’m Autistic and ADHD (AuDHD), and things started
making a lot more sense.</p>
<p>Then I found out that we have all been using the quote wrong.</p>
<p>The full quote is often expressed as “A jack of all trades, master of
none,” though it’s not the entire saying. Interestingly, the complete
version ends on a more positive note:</p>
<blockquote>
<p><em>“A jack of all trades, master of none, but oftentimes better than
a master of one.”</em></p>
</blockquote>
<p>This extended version suggests that having a wide range of skills or
knowledge can be more advantageous than being an expert in just one
area, highlighting the value of versatility and adaptability.</p>
<p>With that in mind, I have asked ChatGPT-4 to write a lesson
plan/course “using the seven basics as a template, create an
instructional plan to teach me (1) the seven basics and (2) how these
basics apply in Python and SQL.”</p>
<p>This instructional plan is designed to teach the seven basics of
coding with an emphasis on applications in Python and SQL over an 8-week
course. Each week focuses on different core concepts, combining
theoretical background with practical exercises.</p>
<p>By mastering these basics, and combining them with our other life
experiences, we can become tailor fit solutions for the ever growing
marketplace of ideas.</p>
<p>I have a lot of financial services experience, by adding this data
and programming, I can help financial services companies know their data
better. Someone in medical can transition to helping medical
professionals understand their patient populations or even use
predictive modeling for early detection. The possible combinations of
experiences and skills are endless.</p>
<p>So when it comes to programming, what are we learning in this
specific course?</p>
<div id="section-4" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="the-7-basics" class="section level1">
<h1>The 7 Basics</h1>
<p>The seven fundamentals of coding — variables, math &amp; logic,
input/output, conditions, loops, functions, and sequences &amp; data
structures — are widely recognized concepts in the field of computer
programming. They are not specific to any one individual or copyrighted
material. These concepts form the core foundation of learning to code in
virtually any programming language and are taught universally in
computer science education and programming tutorials.</p>
</div>
<div id="what-are-the-seven-basics-of-coding" class="section level1">
<h1>What are the seven basics of coding?</h1>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li>Variables</li>
<li>Math &amp; Logic</li>
<li>Input/Output</li>
<li>Conditions</li>
<li>Loops</li>
<li>Functions</li>
<li>Sequences &amp; Data Structures</li>
</ol>
<p>These seven principles offer limitless possibilities for combination.
By mastering them, you gain the ability to recreate any program that
exists, across all programming languages, including those that have not
been invented yet.</p>
<div id="defining-the-seven-basics" class="section level2">
<h2>Defining the seven basics</h2>
<p>Variables: Storage locations in code that hold data which can be
changed during program execution. They are named so programmers can
easily identify and manipulate their values.</p>
<p>Math &amp; Logic: The mathematical operations (such as addition,
subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (like AND,
OR, NOT) that allow manipulation of variables and control the flow of
the program based on conditions.</p>
<p>Input/Output: The mechanism by which a program receives data from the
outside world (input), processes it, and then sends data back to the
outside world (output). Examples include reading user input from a
keyboard and displaying messages on a screen.</p>
<p>Conditions: Statements in code that execute different actions based
on whether a specified condition is true or false. They are fundamental
for decision-making in programming.</p>
<p>Loops: Constructs that repeat a block of code multiple times until a
certain condition is met. They are used to automate repetitive
tasks.</p>
<p>Functions: Named blocks of code designed to perform a specific task.
Functions can take inputs, process them, and return a result. They help
in making code reusable and organized.</p>
<p>Sequences &amp; Data Structures: Organized collections of data that
can be manipulated efficiently. Sequences refer to ordered collections
where each item has a specific position, while data structures are more
complex organizations of data for efficient access and modification
(e.g., arrays, lists, trees, and graphs).</p>
</div>
<div id="section-5" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-1-introduction-and-variables" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 1: Introduction and Variables</h1>
<div id="objectives" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Understand variables in Python and SQL.</li>
<li>Introduction to Python and SQL basics.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Introduction to Python.</li>
<li>Working with variables: declaring, assigning values.</li>
<li>Understanding data types and conversions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Introduction to databases and SQL.</li>
<li>Understanding tables, fields, and variables in SQL queries.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Create Python scripts that use variables.</li>
<li>Write simple SQL queries to manipulate data.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-2-math-logic" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 2: Math &amp; Logic</h1>
<div id="objectives-1" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Learn to apply mathematical and logical operations in Python and
SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-1" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Performing arithmetic operations.</li>
<li>Using logical operators and expressions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-1" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Arithmetic operations in SQL.</li>
<li>Logical operators in WHERE clauses.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-1" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Solve math problems with Python.</li>
<li>Filter data using arithmetic and logical operations in SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-3-inputoutput" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 3: Input/Output</h1>
<div id="objectives-2" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Manage I/O in Python.</li>
<li>Data retrieval and insertion in SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-2" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Reading and writing to files.</li>
<li>User input and console output.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-2" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Inserting and retrieving data with SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-2" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>I/O operations in Python.</li>
<li>CRUD operations in SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-4-conditions" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 4: Conditions</h1>
<div id="objectives-3" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Use conditional statements in Python and SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-3" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>if, elif, and else statements.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-3" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>CASE statements and complex conditions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-3" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Conditional logic in Python scripts.</li>
<li>Conditional logic in SQL queries.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-5-loops" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 5: Loops</h1>
<div id="objectives-4" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Automate tasks with loops in Python. Understand loops or their
alternatives in SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-4" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>for and while loops.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-4" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Cursors and row-by-row operations (with caution).</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-4" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Data processing loops in Python.</li>
<li>Set-based operations in SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-6-functions" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 6: Functions</h1>
<div id="objectives-5" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Modularize code with functions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-5" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Defining and calling functions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-5" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Creating stored procedures and functions.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-5" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Function usage in Python.</li>
<li>Stored procedures in SQL for common operations.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-7-sequences-data-structures" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 7: Sequences &amp; Data Structures</h1>
<div id="objectives-6" class="section level2">
<h2>Objectives</h2>
<ul>
<li>Work with data structures in Python and relational models in
SQL.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="python-section-6" class="section level2">
<h2>Python Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Lists, dictionaries, and sets.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="sql-section-6" class="section level2">
<h2>SQL Section</h2>
<ul>
<li>Join operations and subqueries.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="exercises-6" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ul>
<li>Manipulate data structures in Python.</li>
<li>Complex SQL queries for data retrieval.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-8-integration-and-project-work" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 8: Integration and Project Work</h1>
<div id="objective" class="section level2">
<h2>Objective</h2>
<ul>
<li>Integrate learned concepts in a comprehensive project.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="project-description" class="section level2">
<h2>Project Description</h2>
<ul>
<li>Develop a Python application that interacts with a SQL
database.</li>
<li>Incorporate variables, control structures, functions, and data
structures.</li>
<li>Perform CRUD operations in SQL from Python.</li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="conclusion" class="section level1">
<h1>Conclusion</h1>
<p>This plan outlines a structured approach to learning programming
fundamentals, tailored for practical applications in Python and SQL. By
the end of this course, students will have a solid foundation in coding
basics and the ability to apply these concepts in real-world
scenarios.</p>
<div id="section-6" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-0-prep-set-up" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 0: Prep &amp; Set Up</h1>
<p>Before we even begin, you are going to need some tools. I’m going to
give some options.</p>
<p>For those looking for a more entry level starter way to use this
tutorial, here are some options.</p>
<p>Most of what we are going to do, you can just use the W3 Schools code
editor to accomplish:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Python: <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/python/trypython.asp?filename=demo_default" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/python/trypython.asp?filename=demo_default</a></p></li>
<li><p>SQL: <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/sql/trysql.asp?filename=trysql_select_all" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/sql/trysql.asp?filename=trysql_select_all</a></p></li>
</ul>
<div id="alternate-options" class="section level2">
<h2>Alternate Options</h2>
<p>If you are advanced already, you probably already use Visual Studio
or RStudio or another coding program or code book. Feel free. I prefer
RStudio, as it is way more user friendly. If you want to go that route,
by all means, download RSTudio: <a href="https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/" class="uri">https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/</a>. If you go
this route, make sure to install R and RStudio (see the website for
details).</p>
<p>You can also clone your GitHub repos in RStudio. We can follow each
other on GitHub: <a href="https://github.com/darrellwolfe" class="uri">https://github.com/darrellwolfe</a></p>
<div id="python" class="section level3">
<h3>PYTHON</h3>
<p>Another way to code Python in-browser is</p>
<p>Python: <a href="https://trinket.io/turtle" class="uri">https://trinket.io/turtle</a></p>
<p>Python 3: <a href="https://trinket.io/features/python3" class="uri">https://trinket.io/features/python3</a></p>
<p>ANOTHER way to try Python, and a very popular option, is Jupiter
Notebook: <a href="https://jupyter.org/try-jupyter/notebooks/?path=notebooks/Intro.ipynb" class="uri">https://jupyter.org/try-jupyter/notebooks/?path=notebooks/Intro.ipynb</a></p>
</div>
<div id="sql" class="section level3">
<h3>SQL</h3>
<p>Another way to code-practice SQL in-browser is:</p>
<p>myCompiler: <a href="https://www.mycompiler.io/new/sql" class="uri">https://www.mycompiler.io/new/sql</a></p>
<p>SQLite: <a href="https://sqliteonline.com/" class="uri">https://sqliteonline.com/</a></p>
<p>Also check out Codecademy <a href="https://www.codecademy.com/article/running-sql-commands-in-db-browser" class="uri">https://www.codecademy.com/article/running-sql-commands-in-db-browser</a></p>
<p>But these only really let you type code and see the colors change, it
takes extra work to actually use the and see the code work.</p>
<p>One awesome tool that will let you actually use SQL is Google’s <a href="https://cloud.google.com/bigquery?_gl=1*w88z2n*_up*MQ..&amp;gclid=93d8958c87b81fd32430f384585c7524&amp;gclsrc=3p.ds#cloud-data-warehouse-to-power-your-data-driven-innovation">BigQuery
(link)</a>.</p>
<p>From their website: “BigQuery’s serverless architecture lets you use
SQL queries to analyze your data. You can store and analyze your data
within BigQuery or use BigQuery to assess your data where it lives. To
test how it works for yourself, query data—without a credit card—using
the BigQuery sandbox.”</p>
<p>BigQuery Overview: <a href="https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/introduction" class="uri">https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/introduction</a></p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-7" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-1-introduction-and-variables-1" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 1: Introduction and Variables</h1>
<p>This week, we begin our journey into the world of coding by exploring
the concept of variables. We will look at how variables function in both
Python and SQL, setting the foundation for more complex operations.</p>
<p>Open the W3 schools links (or whatever program you’ve decided to
use):</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Python: <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/python/trypython.asp?filename=demo_default" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/python/trypython.asp?filename=demo_default</a></p></li>
<li><p>SQL: <a href="https://www.w3schools.com/sql/trysql.asp?filename=trysql_select_all" class="uri">https://www.w3schools.com/sql/trysql.asp?filename=trysql_select_all</a></p></li>
</ul>
<div id="section-8" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
<div id="data-types" class="section level2">
<h2>Data Types</h2>
<p>Before we can even start talking about variables, we need to
level-set by discussing data types.</p>
<p>Discussing data types is fundamental for understanding how
information is stored and manipulated in programming languages. Data
types specify the kind of data a variable can hold and determine what
operations can be performed on it. Let’s look at some common data types
in Python and SQL, focusing on strings, integers, and others.</p>
<div id="python-data-types" class="section level3">
<h3>Python Data Types</h3>
<p>In Python, data types are dynamically inferred, which means you don’t
need to declare the type of a variable explicitly. Here are some of the
basic types:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>int: Represents integer values. Example: age = 30</p></li>
<li><p>float: Represents floating-point numbers or decimals. Example:
height = 5.9</p></li>
<li><p>str: Represents text or string data. Example: name =
“Darrell”</p></li>
<li><p>bool: Represents Boolean values (True or False). Example:
is_student = False</p></li>
<li><p>list: An ordered collection of items. Example: colors = [“red”,
“green”, “blue”]</p></li>
<li><p>dict: A collection of key-value pairs. Example: person = {“name”:
“Darrell”, “age”: 30}</p></li>
</ul>
<p>Python also supports other complex data types like tuples, sets, and
custom classes.</p>
</div>
<div id="sql-data-types" class="section level3">
<h3>SQL Data Types</h3>
<p>SQL data types need to be explicitly defined when creating or
altering tables and when declaring variables in scripts or stored
procedures. The exact data types available can vary between different
SQL database systems, but here are some widely used types:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>INT: An integer numeric value. Example: EmployeeID INT (There is
also BIGINT for larger numbers)</p></li>
<li><p>VARCHAR(n) or CHAR(n): Variable-length and fixed-length character
types for strings. VARCHAR is used for strings of varying lengths, and
CHAR for strings of fixed length. Example: FirstName
VARCHAR(50)</p></li>
<li><p>FLOAT or DECIMAL: Used for floating-point numbers and decimals
with precision. Example: Salary DECIMAL(10, 2)</p></li>
<li><p>DATE, TIME, DATETIME: Represent dates, times, and both,
respectively. Example: HireDate DATE</p></li>
<li><p>BOOLEAN: Represents Boolean values, though some SQL systems use
BIT, with 1 for true and 0 for false. Example: IsActive BOOLEAN</p></li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="key-points" class="section level3">
<h3>Key Points</h3>
<ul>
<li><p>Data types are crucial for defining how data is stored and what
operations can be performed on it.</p></li>
<li><p>Python infers data types dynamically and supports a wide range of
built-in types for various applications.</p></li>
<li><p>SQL requires explicit declaration of data types, with some
variation across different database systems, emphasizing the structured
nature of data in databases.</p></li>
</ul>
<p>Understanding these data types and their appropriate use cases is
essential for effective programming and data manipulation, enabling the
development of more robust and error-free applications.</p>
<p>But as you can see already by comparing just two languages, Python
and SQL, the data types are widely crossover fro</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-9" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
<div id="understanding-variables-in-python-and-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Understanding Variables in Python and SQL</h2>
<p>Now we need to talk about what variables are (using Python &amp;
SQL):</p>
<p>Variables are essential in any programming or scripting language.
They act as placeholders or containers for storing data values. In
programming, variables enable us to write flexible and dynamic code,
where we can manipulate data in various ways. Let’s explore what
variables are in the context of Python and SQL, two widely used
languages in software development and database management.</p>
</div>
<div id="section-1-introduction-to-python-variables" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Introduction to Python Variables</h2>
<p>Variables are fundamental in any programming language. They allow us
to store information that can be reused throughout our programs. Let’s
start with Python.</p>
<div id="variables-in-python" class="section level3">
<h3>Variables in Python</h3>
<p>In Python, a variable is created the moment you first assign a value
to it. Variables do not need to be declared with any specific type, and
they can even change type after they have been set.</p>
<p>A variable is essentially a name that is used to refer to some memory
location in the computer, where you can store data values. You can think
of it as a box where you can store something for use later in your code.
Python is dynamically typed, which means you don’t explicitly declare
the data type of a variable. The Python interpreter infers the type
based on the value assigned to the variable.</p>
<p>Here’s a quick example:</p>
<pre class="python"><code>name = &quot;Darrell&quot;  # String variable
age = 30  # Integer variable
is_analyst = True  # Boolean variable</code></pre>
<p>In these examples: - name, age, and is_analyst are variables. -
“Darrell”, 30, and True are the values stored in those variables.</p>
<p>Python understands the type of data (string, integer, boolean)
automatically.</p>
<div id="creating-and-using-variables" class="section level4">
<h4>Creating and Using Variables</h4>
<pre class="python"><code># Assigning values to variables
name = &quot;Jimmy&quot;  # A string variable
age = 30  # An integer variable
height = 6.7  # A floating-point variable

# Using variables
print(f&quot;{name} is {age} years old and {height} feet tall.&quot;)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Jimmy is 30 years old and 6.7 feet tall.</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="characteristics-of-python-variables" class="section level4">
<h4>Characteristics of Python Variables:</h4>
<ul>
<li><p>Dynamically Typed: You don’t explicitly declare the data type of
a variable. The Python interpreter infers the type based on the assigned
value.</p></li>
<li><p>Mutable: The value or type of a variable can change over
time.</p></li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<div id="variables-in-sql" class="section level3">
<h3>Variables in SQL</h3>
<p>SQL (Structured Query Language) variables are a bit different. They
are used within the scope of SQL scripts, stored procedures, or queries
to hold temporary data. In SQL, you typically need to declare a variable
explicitly before using it, including specifying its data type.</p>
<div id="declaring-and-using-variables-in-sql" class="section level4">
<h4>Declaring and Using Variables in SQL</h4>
<p>Here’s how you might declare and use variables in an SQL environment
(example in T-SQL, used by Microsoft SQL Server):</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @EmployeeName VARCHAR(50);

-- Assigning a value to the variable
SET @EmployeeName = &#39;Jane Doe&#39;;

-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 

FROM Employees 

WHERE Name = @EmployeeName;
</code></pre>
<p>A more common example of a SQL variable I use when working with local
government data is dates.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @TaxYear INT = &#39;20240101&#39;;

-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 
FROM CadasterValues 

WHERE TaxYear = @TaxYear;</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="characteristics-of-sql-variables" class="section level4">
<h4>Characteristics of SQL Variables:</h4>
<ul>
<li>Explicit Declaration: Variables must be declared before use,
including specifying their data type.</li>
<li>Scoped: The visibility and lifetime of variables are usually limited
to the batch, stored procedure, or script where they are declared.</li>
<li>Type-Specific: When declaring a variable, you must specify its data
type.</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-1" class="section level4">
<h4>Conclusion</h4>
<p>Variables are a fundamental aspect of both programming and scripting
languages, acting as the basic units for data storage and manipulation.
While Python variables are versatile and dynamically typed, SQL
variables require explicit declaration and are type-specific, reflecting
the structured nature of database languages. Understanding how to
effectively use variables in both Python and SQL is crucial for
developing flexible, efficient, and dynamic applications and
queries.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-10" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
<div id="practice-declaring-variables" class="section level3">
<h3>Practice Declaring Variables</h3>
<p>In the W3 Schools code editer, paste the following two import scripts
and the first example of declaring a variable.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python setup for using Python in R Markdown
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="declaring-variables" class="section level3">
<h3>Declaring Variables</h3>
<p>In Python, variables do not need explicit declaration to reserve
memory space. The declaration happens automatically when you assign a
value to a variable.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Assigning values to variables
x = 5
y = &quot;Hello, World!&quot;
print(x)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 5</code></pre>
<pre class="python"><code>print(y)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Hello, World!</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="variable-types" class="section level3">
<h3>Variable Types</h3>
<p>Python has various data types including integers, float (decimal
numbers), strings, and more. Python’s flexibility allows you to reassign
variables to different data types.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Reassigning variables to different types
x = 5.5   # x is now a float
print(x)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 5.5</code></pre>
<pre class="python"><code>y = True  # y is now a boolean
print(y)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## True</code></pre>
<p>Now play with these and come up with some of your own examples,
change them and see what happns.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Assigning values to variables
x = 100 * 5
y = &quot;You have GOT to be kidding me!&quot;
# There are 43,560 square feet in an acre, multiply the acres by this for the square feet
# If I have 0.56 acres, how many square feet do I have?
z = 0.56
SqFt = z * 43560

print(x)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 500</code></pre>
<pre class="python"><code>print(y)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## You have GOT to be kidding me!</code></pre>
<pre class="python"><code>print(SqFt)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 24393.600000000002</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-introduction-to-sql-variables" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Introduction to SQL Variables</h2>
<p>In SQL, variables are used differently depending on the database
system. For this example, we’ll use T-SQL syntax, common in Microsoft
SQL Server.</p>
<div id="declaring-variables-1" class="section level3">
<h3>Declaring Variables</h3>
<p>Variables in SQL must be declared before use. They are typically used
within scripts or stored procedures.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @EmployeeID int;
-- Assigning a value to the variable
SET @EmployeeID = 1;
-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 
FROM Employees 
WHERE EmployeeID = @EmployeeID;</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="variable-types-1" class="section level3">
<h3>Variable Types</h3>
<p>SQL variables can be of different data types, such as INTEGER,
VARCHAR (variable-length character string), and DATE. The data type of a
variable dictates the kind of data it can store.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Declaring variables of different types
DECLARE @StartDate DATE;
DECLARE @EmployeeName VARCHAR(50);
-- Assigning values
SET @StartDate = &#39;2021-01-01&#39;;
SET @EmployeeName = &#39;John Doe&#39;;</code></pre>
<p>Now try this in W3 Schools</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>Select *
From Customers;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="note-the-first-column-should-not-have-a-comma-before-the-rest-do-and-no-comma-after-the-last-column.-there-are-two-ways-to-accomplish-this." class="section level3">
<h3>Note: The first column should not have a comma before, the rest do,
and no comma after the last column. There are two ways to accomplish
this.</h3>
<p>Most SQL examples and tutorials will show you comma after. This
works, it is the standard way. But what if I want to delete the
“Country” column? Then I have to also remember to remove the comma after
“City”. This isn’t hard, but in long code it gets tedious as you play
with the data. So I use the alternate method.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>Select
CustomerID, -- No comma before CustomerID
CustomerName,
ContactName,
Address,
City,
Country -- Note how there is no comma here

From Customers;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="select-alternate-method" class="section level3">
<h3>Select alternate method:</h3>
<p>Notice how the actual comma is the same. No comma before CustomerID,
no comma after Country. But in this structure, I can delete the country
line without affecting the code performance or having to remember to
alter commas. Trust me, wth lots of movement in your scripts, try this
method.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>Select
CustomerID -- Still no comma before CustomerID
,CustomerName
,ContactName
,Address
,City
,Country -- Note how there is  still no comma here

From Customers;
</code></pre>
<p>Practice using a variable, though this didn’t work in W3 Schools, you
may have to use BigQuery or get another database access to practice
SQL</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>
DECLARE @ID VARCHAR(20) = &#39;3&#39;;

Select
CustomerID
,CustomerName
,ContactName
,Address
,City
,Country

From Customers

Where CustomerID = @ID;
</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="exercises-7" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li><p>In Python, create a variable to store your favorite number and
print it. Write a Python function that takes a name as a parameter and
prints a greeting message.</p></li>
<li><p>In SQL, declare a variable to hold a date and write a query to
select records after this date from a table of your choice.</p></li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-2" class="section level2">
<h2>Conclusion</h2>
<p>Understanding variables is the first step in mastering programming.
This week, we’ve seen how variables are used in both Python and SQL.
Practice the exercises to reinforce what you’ve learned.</p>
<p>This R Markdown document template for Week 1 introduces variables in
both Python and SQL, incorporating code chunks that allow for direct
execution of Python and SQL code if you’re using an appropriate
environment that supports these languages (note that for SQL, the
environment would need to be configured to connect to a SQL database).
Remember to adjust the SQL syntax according to the specific SQL dialect
you’re using (the example uses T-SQL as an illustration).</p>
</div>
<div id="section-11" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-2-math-logic-in-python-and-sql" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 2: Math &amp; Logic in Python and SQL</h1>
<p>In Week 2, we dive into math and logic operations, essential tools
for any programming task. We’ll explore how these operations are
executed in Python and SQL, enhancing our ability to perform
calculations and make decisions in our code.</p>
<div id="section-1-math-logic-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Math &amp; Logic in Python</h2>
<p>Python provides operators to perform mathematical calculations and
logical operations. Let’s look at how to use these to manipulate data
and control the flow of our programs.</p>
<div id="mathematical-operations" class="section level3">
<h3>Mathematical Operations</h3>
<p>Python supports all basic arithmetic operations. Here are some
examples:</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Mathematical operations in Python
addition = 5 + 3
subtraction = 10 - 2
multiplication = 4 * 7
division = 20 / 4
modulus = 18 % 5
print(&quot;Addition:&quot;, addition)
print(&quot;Subtraction:&quot;, subtraction)
print(&quot;Multiplication:&quot;, multiplication)
print(&quot;Division:&quot;, division)
print(&quot;Modulus:&quot;, modulus)</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="logical-operations" class="section level3">
<h3>Logical Operations</h3>
<p>Logical operations in Python are used to compare values. These
operations return either True or False.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Logical operations in Python
a = 5
b = 3
print(&quot;a &gt; b:&quot;, a &gt; b)
print(&quot;a &lt; b:&quot;, a &lt; b)
print(&quot;a == b:&quot;, a == b)
print(&quot;a != b:&quot;, a != b)</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-math-logic-in-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Math &amp; Logic in SQL</h2>
<p>SQL also allows for mathematical and logical operations, particularly
useful in queries to filter, calculate, or manipulate data.</p>
<div id="mathematical-operations-in-sql" class="section level3">
<h3>Mathematical Operations in SQL</h3>
<p>SQL supports basic arithmetic operations directly in the SELECT
statement or WHERE clause.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>
-- Example of mathematical operations in SQL
SELECT 
  EmployeeID,
  (YearlySalary / 12) AS MonthlySalary,
  (YearlySalary * 1.05) AS NextYearProjectedSalary
FROM Employees;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="logical-operations-in-sql" class="section level3">
<h3>Logical Operations in SQL</h3>
<p>Logical operations in SQL are primarily used in the WHERE clause to
filter data based on certain conditions.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>Copy code
-- Example of logical operations in SQL
SELECT * FROM Orders
WHERE OrderDate &gt;= &#39;2022-01-01&#39;
AND OrderDate &lt;= &#39;2022-12-31&#39;;</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-8" class="section level3">
<h3>Exercises</h3>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li><p>In Python, create a simple calculator that takes two numbers as
input and performs addition, subtraction, multiplication, and
division.</p></li>
<li><p>Write a Python script that compares two variables and prints out
a message based on their relation (greater than, less than, equal
to).</p></li>
<li><p>In SQL, write a query to select all employees who have a yearly
salary greater than $50,000 and have been with the company for more than
5 years.</p></li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-3" class="section level3">
<h3>Conclusion</h3>
<p>Understanding and utilizing math and logic operations are fundamental
in programming. This week, we’ve built on our foundational knowledge of
variables by adding the ability to perform calculations and make
decisions in both Python and SQL. Practice these operations to become
more fluent in handling data within your programs.</p>
<p>This document provides an introductory exploration into mathematical
and logical operations in Python and SQL, suited for learners advancing
from basic variables to more complex expressions and data
manipulation.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-12" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-3-inputoutput-in-python-and-data-manipulation-in-sql" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 3: Input/Output in Python and Data Manipulation in SQL</h1>
<p>This week, we’re going to explore input/output operations in Python,
allowing our programs to interact with users and files. In SQL, we’ll
focus on data retrieval and insertion, critical skills for working with
databases.</p>
<div id="section-1-inputoutput-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Input/Output in Python</h2>
<p>Python’s input/output capabilities enable us to read from and write
to files, as well as interact with users via input.</p>
<div id="reading-user-input" class="section level3">
<h3>Reading User Input</h3>
<p>Python uses the <code>input()</code> function to capture user input.
Let’s see it in action.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for capturing user input
user_name = input(&quot;Enter your name: &quot;)
print(&quot;Hello, &quot; + user_name + &quot;!&quot;)</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="reading-and-writing-files" class="section level3">
<h3>Reading and Writing Files</h3>
<p>Reading from and writing to files are common operations. Here’s how
to manage files in Python.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for reading from and writing to a file
# Writing to a file
with open(&#39;example.txt&#39;, &#39;w&#39;) as file:
    file.write(&quot;Hello, Python file handling!&quot;)

# Reading from a file
with open(&#39;example.txt&#39;, &#39;r&#39;) as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-data-manipulation-in-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Data Manipulation in SQL</h2>
<p>Managing data is a fundamental aspect of SQL, including retrieving,
inserting, updating, and deleting records.</p>
<div id="retrieving-data" class="section level3">
<h3>Retrieving Data</h3>
<p>The SELECT statement is used to query data from a database.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for retrieving data
SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID = 3;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="inserting-data" class="section level3">
<h3>Inserting Data</h3>
<p>To add new records to a table, we use the INSERT INTO statement.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for inserting data
INSERT INTO Employees (FirstName, LastName, DepartmentID)
VALUES (&#39;John&#39;, &#39;Doe&#39;, 3);
</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="exercises-9" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li><p>Write a Python program that asks the user for their favorite
number, then saves this number to a file. Read the number back from the
file and print it to the console.</p></li>
<li><p>Create a Python script that reads a file line by line and prints
each line to the console.</p></li>
<li><p>In SQL, write a query to insert a new record into a table of your
choice, then write a query to retrieve this record.</p></li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-4" class="section level2">
<h2>Conclusion</h2>
<p>Interacting with the outside world through input and output
operations in Python and manipulating data in SQL are crucial for
creating dynamic and responsive applications. Practice these skills to
improve your ability to create more complex and interactive
programs.</p>
<p>This document for Week 3 introduces the learners to essential I/O
operations in Python and fundamental data manipulation techniques in
SQL. It provides a hands-on approach with exercises designed to
reinforce the concepts discussed.</p>
</div>
<div id="section-13" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-4-conditional-statements-in-python-and-sql" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 4: Conditional Statements in Python and SQL</h1>
<p>Week 4 delves into conditional statements, a critical component of
programming that allows the execution of code based on specific
conditions. We’ll explore how to implement these logical structures in
Python and SQL.</p>
<div id="section-1-conditional-statements-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Conditional Statements in Python</h2>
<p>Conditional statements in Python enable decision-making in programs,
allowing for different outcomes based on varying conditions.</p>
<div id="if-elif-and-else-statements" class="section level3">
<h3>if, elif, and else Statements</h3>
<p>These statements control the flow of execution based on the
truthiness of conditions.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating if, elif, and else
x = 10
if x &gt; 5:
    print(&quot;x is greater than 5&quot;)
elif x &lt; 5:
    print(&quot;x is less than 5&quot;)
else:
    print(&quot;x is exactly 5&quot;)</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="logical-operators" class="section level2">
<h2>Logical Operators</h2>
<p>Python supports logical operators such as and, or, and not for
combining conditions.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for logical operators
a = True
b = False
if a and not b:
    print(&quot;a is True and b is False&quot;)</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="section-2-conditional-logic-in-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Conditional Logic in SQL</h2>
<p>In SQL, conditional logic is often applied in queries to filter data
or in the use of CASE statements for more complex conditional
outcomes.</p>
<div id="filtering-with-where-clause" class="section level3">
<h3>Filtering with WHERE Clause</h3>
<p>The WHERE clause in SQL uses conditions to filter records.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for filtering data with WHERE clause
SELECT * FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID = 3 AND Salary &gt; 50000;
</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="using-case-statements" class="section level2">
<h2>Using CASE Statements</h2>
<p>The CASE statement in SQL allows for conditional logic in query
results.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for CASE statements
SELECT EmployeeID, FirstName, LastName,
    CASE 
        WHEN Salary &gt; 60000 THEN &#39;High&#39;
        WHEN Salary BETWEEN 40000 AND 60000 THEN &#39;Medium&#39;
        ELSE &#39;Low&#39;
    END AS SalaryRange
FROM Employees;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-10" class="section level2">
<h2>Exercises</h2>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li>Write a Python program that asks the user to enter a number. Use
conditional statements to print whether the number is positive,
negative, or zero.</li>
<li>Create a Python script that uses logical operators to check multiple
conditions and outputs a result based on these conditions.</li>
<li>In SQL, write a query that uses a CASE statement to categorize
employees by their salary ranges (e.g., Low, Medium, High).</li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-5" class="section level2">
<h2>Conclusion</h2>
<p>Conditional statements are fundamental to programming, enabling
dynamic decision-making based on different conditions. By mastering
conditionals in both Python and SQL, you can write more flexible and
powerful code that responds to various scenarios effectively.</p>
<p>This Week 4 document offers a comprehensive guide on implementing
conditional logic in Python and SQL. It’s designed to build on the
foundational skills taught in previous weeks, gradually increasing the
learner’s ability to handle more complex programming tasks.</p>
</div>
<div id="section-14" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-5-mastering-loops-in-python-and-sql" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 5: Mastering Loops in Python and SQL</h1>
<p>In Week 5, we delve into the concept of loops, a fundamental
programming construct that allows for the execution of a block of code
repeatedly based on a condition. We’ll explore how to use loops in
Python for various iterative tasks and introduce how similar iterative
processes can be managed in SQL, focusing on set-based operations as SQL
does not have traditional loop constructs like Python.</p>
<div id="section-1-loops-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Loops in Python</h2>
<p>Loops in Python are powerful, allowing you to iterate over items of
any sequence including lists, strings, or even ranges of numbers.</p>
<div id="for-loops" class="section level3">
<h3>for Loops</h3>
<p>The <code>for</code> loop in Python is used to iterate over elements
of a sequence.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating a for loop
for i in range(5):
    print(&quot;Python iteration number:&quot;, i)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Python iteration number: 0
## Python iteration number: 1
## Python iteration number: 2
## Python iteration number: 3
## Python iteration number: 4</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="while-loops" class="section level3">
<h3>while Loops</h3>
<p>The while loop executes as long as the provided condition is
true.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating a while loop
count = 0
while count &lt; 5:
    print(&quot;While loop count:&quot;, count)
    count += 1</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-iterative-processing-in-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Iterative Processing in SQL</h2>
<p>Since SQL is a set-based language, it does not use loops in the way
procedural programming languages do. However, certain SQL extensions
like PL/SQL or T-SQL provide loop-like constructs for complex
operations.</p>
<div id="iterating-with-cursors-in-sql-use-sparingly" class="section level3">
<h3>Iterating with Cursors in SQL (Use Sparingly)</h3>
<p>Cursors can iterate over database rows, but they should be used
sparingly due to performance concerns.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Example SQL code chunk for cursor (T-SQL syntax)
DECLARE @EmployeeName VARCHAR(50);
DECLARE employee_cursor CURSOR FOR
SELECT FirstName FROM Employees;
OPEN employee_cursor;
FETCH NEXT FROM employee_cursor INTO @EmployeeName;
WHILE @@FETCH_STATUS = 0
BEGIN
    PRINT @EmployeeName;
    FETCH NEXT FROM employee_cursor INTO @EmployeeName;
END;
CLOSE employee_cursor;
DEALLOCATE employee_cursor;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="preferred-set-based-operations" class="section level3">
<h3>Preferred: Set-Based Operations</h3>
<p>SQL is designed for set-based operations, which are more efficient
than row-by-row processing.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Example SQL code chunk for set-based operation
SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employees WHERE DepartmentID = 3;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-11" class="section level3">
<h3>Exercises</h3>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li>Write a Python script that uses a for loop to iterate over a list of
numbers, printing each number squared.</li>
<li>Create a Python program that uses a while loop to ask the user for
input repeatedly until they type “exit”.</li>
<li>In SQL, if your dialect supports it, write a script using a cursor
to iterate over a table and select names. Reflect on why a set-based
approach might be preferred.</li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-6" class="section level3">
<h3>Conclusion</h3>
<p>Loops in Python provide a straightforward way to execute code
multiple times, essential for tasks that require repetition. In SQL,
while direct looping constructs exist in certain dialects, leveraging
set-based operations is generally more efficient and aligns with SQL’s
design philosophy. Understanding these concepts will enhance your
ability to automate and optimize data processing tasks in both
languages.</p>
<p>This Week 5 document introduces loops in Python and discusses how
iterative processes are handled in SQL, emphasizing the strengths and
limitations of loops within each context.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-15" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-6-functions-in-python-and-stored-procedures-in-sql" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 6: Functions in Python and Stored Procedures in SQL</h1>
<p>In Week 6, we’ll explore the use of functions in Python and stored
procedures and functions in SQL. These constructs allow for modularizing
code into reusable blocks, enhancing maintainability and
scalability.</p>
<div id="section-1-functions-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Functions in Python</h2>
<p>Functions in Python are defined using the <code>def</code> keyword
and can accept parameters and return values.</p>
<div id="defining-and-calling-functions" class="section level3">
<h3>Defining and Calling Functions</h3>
<p>Here’s how to define a simple function in Python that takes an
argument and returns a value.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for defining and calling a function
def greet(name):
    return &quot;Hello, &quot; + name + &quot;!&quot;

# Calling the function
print(greet(&quot;Darrell&quot;))</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Hello, Darrell!</code></pre>
<p>Parameters and Return Values Functions can have multiple parameters
and return values. They can also return multiple values in the form of
tuples.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for a function with multiple parameters and return values
def arithmetic_operations(a, b):
    return a+b, a-b, a*b, a/b

# Calling the function and unpacking return values
addition, subtraction, multiplication, division = arithmetic_operations(10, 5)
print(&quot;Addition:&quot;, addition)
print(&quot;Subtraction:&quot;, subtraction)
print(&quot;Multiplication:&quot;, multiplication)
print(&quot;Division:&quot;, division)</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-stored-procedures-and-functions-in-sql" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Stored Procedures and Functions in SQL</h2>
<p>Stored procedures and functions in SQL allow for encapsulating SQL
queries and commands for reuse and execution.</p>
<div id="creating-and-using-a-stored-procedure" class="section level3">
<h3>Creating and Using a Stored Procedure</h3>
<p>Stored procedures can perform operations such as data manipulation
and return the results.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for creating and using a stored procedure (T-SQL syntax)
CREATE PROCEDURE GetEmployeeInfo
    @EmployeeID INT
AS
BEGIN
    SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE EmployeeID = @EmployeeID;
END;
GO

-- Invoking the stored procedure
EXEC GetEmployeeInfo @EmployeeID = 1;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="creating-and-using-a-function-in-sql" class="section level3">
<h3>Creating and Using a Function in SQL</h3>
<p>Functions in SQL are used to compute values and cannot change the
database state.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for creating and using a function (T-SQL syntax)
CREATE FUNCTION GetFullName (@FirstName VARCHAR(100), @LastName VARCHAR(100))
RETURNS VARCHAR(200)
AS
BEGIN
    RETURN @FirstName + &#39; &#39; + @LastName;
END;
GO

-- Using the function in a SELECT statement
SELECT dbo.GetFullName(FirstName, LastName) AS FullName FROM Employees;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-12" class="section level3">
<h3>Exercises</h3>
<p>Create a Python function that calculates the factorial of a number
and test it with different values. Write a Python function that checks
whether a given word is a palindrome (reads the same backward and
forward).</p>
<p>In SQL, create a stored procedure that retrieves employees by their
department ID and test it. Develop an SQL function that calculates the
yearly salary increase based on current salary and percentage increase
rate.</p>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-7" class="section level3">
<h3>Conclusion</h3>
<p>Functions and stored procedures are crucial for writing clean,
efficient, and reusable code. By encapsulating specific tasks into these
constructs, you can simplify complex operations in both Python and SQL,
making your code more organized and easier to maintain.</p>
<p>This Week 6 document aims to introduce and solidify the understanding
and usage of functions in Python and stored procedures and functions in
SQL, through both explanation and hands-on practice with code
examples.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-16" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-7-sequences-data-structures-and-relational-models" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 7: Sequences, Data Structures, and Relational Models</h1>
<p>Week 7 dives into more complex types of data management: sequences
and data structures in Python for organizing and manipulating data, and
the relational data model in SQL that underpins how data is stored,
accessed, and manipulated in databases.</p>
<div id="section-1-sequences-and-data-structures-in-python" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 1: Sequences and Data Structures in Python</h2>
<p>Python offers a variety of data structures for efficiently storing
and managing data. Let’s explore lists, dictionaries, and sets.</p>
<div id="lists" class="section level3">
<h3>Lists</h3>
<p>Lists in Python are ordered sequences that can hold a variety of
object types. They support indexing, slicing, and a host of methods for
manipulation.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating lists
my_list = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(my_list[0])  # Accessing the first element</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 1</code></pre>
<pre class="python"><code>my_list.append(6)  # Adding an element
print(my_list)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="dictionaries" class="section level3">
<h3>Dictionaries</h3>
<p>Dictionaries store key-value pairs and are ideal for fast lookup of
values by key.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating dictionaries
my_dict = {&#39;name&#39;: &#39;Darrell&#39;, &#39;age&#39;: 30}
print(my_dict[&#39;name&#39;])  # Accessing value by key
my_dict[&#39;profession&#39;] = &#39;Data Analyst&#39;  # Adding a new key-value pair
print(my_dict)</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="sets" class="section level3">
<h3>Sets</h3>
<p>Sets are unordered collections of unique elements, useful for
operations like union, intersection, and difference.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for demonstrating sets
my_set = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
print(my_set)
my_set.add(6)  # Adding a new element
print(my_set)</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-2-relational-data-model-and-sql-operations" class="section level2">
<h2>Section 2: Relational Data Model and SQL Operations</h2>
<p>The relational data model organizes data into tables (relations) that
relate to each other through foreign keys.</p>
<div id="join-operations" class="section level3">
<h3>JOIN Operations</h3>
<p>JOIN operations in SQL allow for the combination of rows from two or
more tables based on a related column between them.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for demonstrating JOIN operations
SELECT Employees.Name, Departments.Name
FROM Employees
JOIN Departments ON Employees.DepartmentID = Departments.ID;
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="subqueries" class="section level3">
<h3>Subqueries</h3>
<p>Subqueries are queries within queries, useful for complex data
retrieval.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- SQL code chunk for demonstrating subqueries
SELECT Name
FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID IN (SELECT ID FROM Departments WHERE Name = &#39;IT&#39;);
</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-13" class="section level3">
<h3>Exercises</h3>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li>Create a Python script that uses lists to store the names of your
favorite movies and then prints them out.</li>
<li>Write a Python program that uses dictionaries to store information
about a book (title, author, year), then prints out the
information.</li>
<li>Use sets in Python to find the common elements between two sets of
numbers.</li>
<li>Write an SQL query using JOIN to retrieve employee names and their
department names.</li>
<li>Craft an SQL query with a subquery to find all employees in a
specific department based on the department name.</li>
</ol>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-8" class="section level3">
<h3>Conclusion</h3>
<p>Understanding and utilizing sequences and data structures in Python
and grasping the relational data model and its operations in SQL are
pivotal for managing complex data. These constructs enhance your ability
to organize, access, and manipulate data effectively, serving as
fundamental skills in both programming and database</p>
<p>This Week 7 document is crafted to enhance the learner’s ability to
work with complex data structures in Python and understand relational
database operations in SQL, bridging the gap between basic programming
concepts and more advanced data manipulation techniques.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-17" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="week-8-integration-and-project-work-1" class="section level1">
<h1>Week 8: Integration and Project Work</h1>
<p>In Week 8, we culminate our learning journey by integrating the
coding fundamentals we’ve explored into a comprehensive project. This
project will involve developing a Python application that interfaces
with a SQL database, showcasing the practical application of variables,
control structures, functions, and data structures in Python, alongside
database manipulation in SQL.</p>
<div id="project-objective" class="section level2">
<h2>Project Objective</h2>
<p>The goal of this project is to create a Python application that
performs CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on a SQL
database. This application will serve as a demonstration of your ability
to apply programming concepts in Python and SQL to solve real-world
problems.</p>
<div id="project-requirements" class="section level3">
<h3>Project Requirements</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Python Application</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Use variables, loops, and conditional statements to navigate the
program flow.</li>
<li>Implement functions to organize your code into logical blocks.</li>
<li>Utilize lists or dictionaries to manage data within the
application.</li>
</ul></li>
<li><strong>SQL Database</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Design a simple database schema with at least one table to store
data.</li>
<li>Use SQL commands to create the table and define its structure.</li>
<li>Perform insert, select, update, and delete operations from your
Python application.</li>
</ul></li>
</ul>
</div>
<div id="suggested-project-ideas" class="section level3">
<h3>Suggested Project Ideas</h3>
<ol style="list-style-type: decimal">
<li><strong>Contact Book Application</strong>: A simple application to
store, retrieve, update, and delete contact information (e.g., name,
phone number, email) in a database.</li>
<li><strong>Inventory Management System</strong>: An application to
manage products in an inventory, including adding new products, updating
stock levels, retrieving product information, and deleting products from
the inventory.</li>
</ol>
</div>
</div>
<div id="implementation-guide" class="section level2">
<h2>Implementation Guide</h2>
<div id="setting-up-the-database" class="section level3">
<h3>Setting Up the Database</h3>
<p>First, design your database schema and create your tables using
SQL.</p>
<pre class="sql"><code>-- Example SQL code for creating a table
CREATE TABLE Contacts (
    ContactID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(100),
    PhoneNumber VARCHAR(15),
    Email VARCHAR(100)
);</code></pre>
</div>
</div>
<div id="developing-the-python-application" class="section level2">
<h2>Developing the Python Application</h2>
<p>Outline the structure of your Python application, defining the
functions needed for each operation.</p>
<div id="connecting-to-the-database" class="section level3">
<h3>Connecting to the Database</h3>
<p>Use a Python library such as sqlite3 or pyodbc to connect to your SQL
database.</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for connecting to a database
import sqlite3

# Connect to SQLite database (or change to connect to another database type)
conn = sqlite3.connect(&#39;mydatabase.db&#39;)</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="crud-operations" class="section level3">
<h3>CRUD Operations</h3>
<p>Implement functions for each CRUD operation. For example, creating a
new contact:</p>
<pre class="python"><code># Python code chunk for inserting data into the database
def add_contact(conn, contact_info):
    sql = &#39;&#39;&#39; INSERT INTO Contacts(Name, PhoneNumber, Email)
              VALUES(?,?,?) &#39;&#39;&#39;
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(sql, contact_info)
    conn.commit()</code></pre>
</div>
<div id="exercises-14" class="section level3">
<h3>Exercises</h3>
<p>Plan the database schema for your project idea. What tables and
fields will you need? Outline the Python functions you will implement to
interact with the database. Begin coding your Python application,
starting with the database connection and a simple interface for
performing the CRUD operations.</p>
</div>
<div id="conclusion-9" class="section level3">
<h3>Conclusion</h3>
<p>This final week’s project is an opportunity to apply everything
you’ve learned throughout this course. By building a Python application
that interacts with a SQL database, you demonstrate not only your
understanding of coding principles but also your ability to apply these
concepts to create functional, real-world applications. Good luck, and
have fun with your project!</p>
<p>This Week 8 document is designed to guide you through the process of
applying the coding fundamentals you’ve learned into a cohesive project,
emphasizing the practical application of programming concepts in a
real-world scenario.</p>
</div>
</div>
<div id="section-18" class="section level2">
<h2>—————————————————————</h2>
<hr />
</div>
</div>
<div id="closing-thoughts" class="section level1">
<h1>Closing thoughts</h1>
<p>You have read through this document as it exists today. At some point
in the future, it may change if I learn new stuff and have the dopamine
driving me to update this document. It is in my
Technical_Documentation_By_DarrellWolfe repo in GitHub if you want to
copy it into your system and make it your own with your own notes. <a href="https://github.com/darrellwolfe/Technical_Documentation_By_DarrellWolfe" class="uri">https://github.com/darrellwolfe/Technical_Documentation_By_DarrellWolfe</a></p>
<p>Close Git Link: <a href="https://github.com/darrellwolfe/Technical_Documentation_By_DarrellWolfe.git" class="uri">https://github.com/darrellwolfe/Technical_Documentation_By_DarrellWolfe.git</a></p>
<p>For now, I leave you with this…</p>
<p>Practice makes better. Reading this will only help a little. Open up
the tools, get to working. Practice things. If there is an error or
something weird on this page, comment on the website or email <a href="mailto:me@darrellwolfe.com" class="email">me@darrellwolfe.com</a>.</p>
<pre class="python"><code>print(&quot;You Got This!&quot;)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## You Got This!</code></pre>
<pre class="sql"><code>
#AuDHD

Select
ygt.You
,ygt.Are
,ygt.Awesome
,ygt.capacity

From YouGotThis AS ygt

Where ygt.capacity LIKE &#39;%Dopamine_Sufficient%&#39;
</code></pre>
<p>Shalom! Namaste! TYL</p>
</div>
<div id="the-end-for-now" class="section level1">
<h1>The End… For Now…</h1>
</div>




</div>
